words.definition: To do or say something to relieve tension or shyness in a group.
words.example: He told a joke to break the ice at the meeting.

words.definition: To force oneself to do or endure something unpleasant.
words.example: She bit the bullet and paid the fine.

words.word: Hit the nail on the head
words.definition: To describe exactly what is causing a situation or problem.
words.example: Your comment hit the nail on the head.

words.definition: Something very easy to do.
words.example: The exam was a piece of cake.

words.definition: Feeling slightly ill.
words.example: I'm staying home; I feel a bit under the weather.

words.word: Cost an arm and a leg
words.definition: To be very expensive.
words.example: Those tickets cost an arm and a leg.

words.definition: Very rarely.
words.example: He visits us once in a blue moon.

words.word: Let the cat out of the bag
words.definition: To reveal a secret, usually by accident.
words.example: She let the cat out of the bag about the party.

words.definition: To disclose secret information.
words.example: Come on, spill the beans!

words.word: Kill two birds with one stone
words.definition: To achieve two aims with a single action.
words.example: By cycling to work I kill two birds with one stone.

words.word: The ball is in your court
words.definition: It is your turn to take action or make a decision.
words.example: I've done my part; the ball is in your court.

words.definition: Pursuing a mistaken line of thought or action.
words.example: If you think I broke it, you're barking up the wrong tree.

words.definition: To avoid getting to the point.
words.example: Stop beating around the bush and tell me.

words.definition: A situation combining the advantages of two different things.
words.example: Working from home gives me the best of both worlds.

words.definition: Something that seems bad at first but proves beneficial.
words.example: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise.

words.definition: To work late into the night.
words.example: She burned the midnight oil to finish the report.

words.definition: To stop working on something.
words.example: Let's call it a day and go home.

words.definition: To be upset about something that cannot be changed.
words.example: No use crying over spilled milk.

words.definition: To do something poorly or cheaply to save time or money.
words.example: Don't cut corners on safety.

words.definition: One who argues an opposing view for the sake of debate.
words.example: Let me play devil's advocate here.

words.definition: An obvious problem no one wants to discuss.
words.example: We must address the elephant in the room.

words.definition: To accept the unpleasant consequences of one's actions.
words.example: He had to face the music after lying.

words.definition: Feeling sad or depressed.
words.example: She's been feeling blue since he left.

words.definition: To become nervous about something planned.
words.example: He got cold feet on his wedding day.

words.word: Give the benefit of the doubt
words.definition: To trust what someone says without proof.
words.example: I'll give her the benefit of the doubt.

words.definition: To make a special effort beyond what is required.
words.example: Our teacher always goes the extra mile.

words.definition: To leave or begin a journey.
words.example: It's late; time to hit the road.

words.definition: To go to bed.
words.example: I'm exhausted; I'm going to hit the sack.

words.definition: In trouble.
words.example: He's in hot water with the boss.

words.definition: To join others in doing something popular.
words.example: Everyone's jumping on the fitness bandwagon.

words.definition: Stay cheerful in a difficult situation.
words.example: Keep your chin up; things will improve.

words.definition: To die (informal, often humorous).
words.example: The old dog finally kicked the bucket.

words.definition: To avoid restarting an old argument.
words.example: Don't mention it — let sleeping dogs lie.

words.definition: To lose an opportunity by being too slow.
words.example: I missed the boat on that job offer.

words.definition: Success requires effort and suffering.
words.example: Keep training — no pain, no gain.

words.definition: Alert and quick to understand.
words.example: The new assistant is really on the ball.

words.definition: After a bad experience, one becomes cautious.
words.example: She won't lend money again — once bitten, twice shy.

words.definition: Suddenly and unexpectedly.
words.example: He called me out of the blue.

words.definition: To tease or joke with someone.
words.example: Relax — I'm just pulling your leg.

words.definition: To spoil someone's plans or enjoyment.
words.example: I hate to rain on your parade, but…

words.definition: To agree with someone.
words.example: We don't always see eye to eye.

words.definition: To avoid taking sides in a dispute.
words.example: Stop sitting on the fence and decide.

words.definition: Said when a person appears just as they are being discussed.
words.example: Speak of the devil — here she comes!

words.word: Take it with a grain of salt
words.definition: To regard something as exaggerated or unreliable.
words.example: Take his advice with a grain of salt.

words.definition: The final problem that makes a situation intolerable.
words.example: Missing dinner was the last straw.

words.definition: To give up or admit defeat.
words.example: After two hours he threw in the towel.

words.definition: To get married.
words.example: They plan to tie the knot in June.

words.definition: To ignore something wrong on purpose.
words.example: The guard turned a blind eye to the noise.

words.definition: Right in front of someone, though not noticed.
words.example: The keys were under my nose the whole time.

words.word: Wear one's heart on one's sleeve
words.definition: To show one's feelings openly.
words.example: He wears his heart on his sleeve.

words.definition: Something that will never happen.
words.example: He'll apologise when pigs fly.

words.definition: Very common and of little value.
words.example: Cheap souvenirs are a dime a dozen here.

words.definition: What one does matters more than what one says.
words.example: Show me — actions speak louder than words.

words.definition: To make a bad situation worse.
words.example: Shouting only added fuel to the fire.

words.definition: Working hurriedly to meet a deadline.
words.example: The team worked against the clock.

words.definition: Fully attentive and eager to listen.
words.example: Tell me your idea — I'm all ears.

words.definition: A person one loves and is very proud of.
words.example: His granddaughter is the apple of his eye.

words.definition: Back to the beginning after a failure.
words.example: The plan collapsed and we're back to square one.

words.definition: A rough numerical estimate.
words.example: Just give me a ball park figure.

words.definition: To try very hard to help someone.
words.example: She bent over backwards to please her guests.

words.word: Bite off more than you can chew
words.definition: To take on more than one can handle.
words.example: He bit off more than he could chew with three jobs.

words.definition: Good luck (said especially to performers).
words.example: Break a leg out there tonight!

words.definition: To destroy relationships or opportunities permanently.
words.example: Don't burn bridges when you resign.

words.definition: Said to someone who is silent when expected to speak.
words.example: Well? Cat got your tongue?

words.definition: Discovered doing something wrong.
words.example: The thief was caught red-handed.

words.definition: A fresh start with no past mistakes counted.
words.example: The new manager gave everyone a clean slate.

words.definition: Deliberately unfriendly treatment.
words.example: She gave him the cold shoulder all evening.

words.definition: No matter what happens.
words.example: We meet every Sunday, come rain or shine.

words.word: Cross that bridge when we come to it
words.definition: To deal with a problem only when it arises.
words.example: Let's cross that bridge when we come to it.

words.definition: To get to the point.
words.example: Cut to the chase — what do you want?

words.definition: Until the very last moment.
words.example: The match went down to the wire.

words.definition: To make a mistake, especially by being careless.
words.example: He dropped the ball on the client's order.

words.word: Every cloud has a silver lining
words.definition: Every difficult situation has a positive aspect.
words.example: Cheer up — every cloud has a silver lining.

words.definition: Honestly and according to the rules.
words.example: We won fair and square.

words.definition: Someone in an unfamiliar, uncomfortable environment.
words.example: At the formal dinner he felt like a fish out of water.

words.definition: In very good health.
words.example: At eighty she's still fit as a fiddle.

words.definition: To become impossible to control.
words.example: The party got out of hand.

words.definition: To fail dramatically.
words.example: The proposal went down in flames.

words.definition: Persevere despite difficulty.
words.example: Hang in there — you'll pass the exam.

words.definition: To study hard.
words.example: Exams start Monday; time to hit the books.

words.definition: Wait — be patient.
words.example: Hold your horses, I'm coming!

words.definition: In the same difficult situation as others.
words.example: We're all in the same boat here.

words.definition: Both parties are responsible for a situation.
words.example: Don't blame him alone — it takes two to tango.

words.definition: To act too soon, before the right time.
words.example: Don't jump the gun before the results are out.

words.definition: To watch carefully.
words.example: Please keep an eye on the children.

words.definition: To learn how something is done.
words.example: Give her time to learn the ropes.

words.definition: To lose an ability one once had.
words.example: The old chef hasn't lost his touch.

words.definition: To tell something briefly.
words.example: To make a long story short, we missed the train.

words.definition: A hidden logic in seemingly strange behaviour.
words.example: There's method to his madness.

words.definition: Very close in a race or contest.
words.example: The two teams are neck and neck.

words.definition: Free from an obligation or blame.
words.example: He's off the hook for now.

words.definition: In a risky situation.
words.example: You're on thin ice with the boss.

words.definition: In a risky, exposed position.
words.example: She went out on a limb to defend him.

words.definition: To improvise as the situation develops.
words.example: We'll just play it by ear tonight.

words.definition: To regain composure.
words.example: Pull yourself together and try again.

words.word: Put all your eggs in one basket
words.definition: To risk everything on a single venture.
words.example: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

words.definition: To find a hidden meaning in something said.
words.example: Read between the lines of his letter.

words.definition: Immediately, without delay.
words.example: She agreed right off the bat.

words.definition: To disturb an existing situation.
words.example: Don't rock the boat before the deal closes.

words.definition: Ordinary; nothing special.
words.example: It was a run-of-the-mill hotel.
